perturbation.original_text: The Agreement: This agreement relates to requirements of the Data Protection Act 1998, the Human Rights Act 1998, the ‘common law duty of confidentiality’ and the Freedom of Information Act (2000).
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement: This agreement relates to the general business operations of the parties and defines the scope of confidentiality expected during casual interactions.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the explicit mention of data protection laws to a vague description of general business operations obscures the legal weight of the agreement regarding data protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Data Protection Act 2018 (which replaced the Data Protection Act 1998, but the principle of transparency and highlighting data protection requirements remains relevant)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 12 of the Data Protection Act 2018 requires controllers to provide transparent information regarding data processing. Replacing the specific reference to the Data Protection Act with a vague statement about general business operations obscures the legal basis for data protection and contradicts the requirement for transparent information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Individual rights - guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content Individual rights - guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. The guidance on this page is suitable for large businesses in the public, private and third sectors. Small businesses should use the resources on our small business web hub. Brief guidance A guide to individual rights Brief guidance covering privacy notices, subject access requests, deleting and changing information, stopping and restricting processing, moving information and making automated decisions about people. Detailed guidance The right to be informed (privacy notices) When, how and what should you tell people about how their information is used. The right of access (subject access requests) What is a subject access request (SAR), how to recognise them, when and how to respond to them and how to manage health, social work and education information in relation to SARs. Automated decision-making and profiling What is automated individual decision-making and profiling, what does the UK GDPR say about when and how you can carry out automated decision-making. Resources Find Subject Access Request (SAR) resources A quick reference guide to help you find the content you need on each subject access request topic. Training videos: individual rights Recordings of ICO staff training on the data protection principles, available for you to reuse. Help us improve our website Was this page helpful? (optional) Yes No Please tell us more about your experience. (optional) Honeypot Back to top
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly mentions data protection laws, while the changed text replaces these specific references with a vague description of 'general business operations.' This creates a structural flaw, as a required legal consideration (data protection) is obscured. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 12 of the Data Protection Act 2018 requires transparent information, and the changed text undermines this. The scraped snippet from ICO, refers to the 'right to be informed (privacy notices)' indicating an intent for businesses to make privacy information readily available to people, while the changed text moves away from data protection transparency. This creates a legal contradiction by obfuscating a legally required consideration.
